Document [3] (Title: Adenoid Cystic Cancer -- Medical Oncology) Because ACCs do not have a fast turnover rate, systemic chemotherapy does not confer benefit generally, and therefore a standard chemotherapy regimen is not a part of treatment for ACCs. [1] However, the consensus remains to reserve chemotherapy for palliative purposes in patients with symptomatic metastatic disease, recurrent disease, or rapidly progressing tumors not amenable to surgery. Studies have also been done to look at possible molecular targets for immunotherapy agents, but results have been disappointing. Clinical trials looking at c-KIT (an onco-receptor overexpressed by ACCs) as a potential target for the c-KIT inhibitor, imatinib, showed disappointing results. [14] [15] Similarly, drugs targeting the EGFR and HER-2 receptor families did not show any clinical benefit. [16] [17] [18] However, a recent phase II trial looking at the tyrosine kinase inhibitor axitinib in patients with incurable recurrent/metastatic ACC showed that 66.7% of patients had tumor shrinkage and 9.1% of patients had a partial response suggesting that patients with 4q12 amplified ACCs may benefit from tyrosine kinase inhibitors. [19] NTRK inhibitors such as larotrectinib should be considered in NTRK mutations in the absence of other available options in recurrent or metastatic tumors, as approved by the FDA.

Document [13] (Title: Reed-Sternberg Cells -- Clinical Significance) A dysregulation, a mutation, of multiple signaling pathways, may also be responsible for the pathogenesis of Hodgkin disease, eg, JAK/STAT and the Nuclear Factor-KB (NF-KB). [16] [17] Recent studies have shown that the JAK/STAT is overactivated in Hodgkin disease and acts as a conduit of signaling, augmentation, and support of the inflammatory TME. [18] Agents, eg, ruxolitinib (an inhibitor of Jak1 and Jak2), have shown some efficacy in Hodgkin cell models. The Anti-GSF receptor (CSF3R) may also be a surrogate marker for this JAK/STAT overactivity. NF-KB drives inflammatory cytokine production (eg, IL6, TNF-alpha, and GMCSF), affects cellular senescence, and provides cancer with prosurvival signals. Studies have also attempted to use Bortesonib (a protease inhibitor) to suppress NF-KB by preventing its IxB component degradation. Unfortunately, these studies show a failure of Bortesonib as a treatment for Hodgkin disease. The resultant belief is that there are different pathways also involved as well.

Document [16] (Title: Nintedanib -- Mechanism of Action) Nintedanib is an indolinone derivative that binds to tyrosine kinase receptors. The mechanism of action is thought to involve the restriction of neoangiogenesis by inhibiting various growth factors such as fibroblast growth factor receptor (FGFR), vascular endothelial growth factor receptor (VEGFR), platelet-derived growth factor receptor (PDGFR), colony-stimulating factor-1-receptor (CSF1R), and Fms-like tyrosine kinase-3 (FLT3). [13] This drug was initially designed to treat non-small lung cancer. Due to similarities in the growth factors involved in the pathogenesis of this cancer and pulmonary fibrosis, nintedanib was considered a potential treatment for IPF and was studied. [14] Nintedanib competitively binds to the intracellular adenosine triphosphate (ATP) binding site of the growth factor receptors, preventing autophosphorylation and blocking downstream signaling cascades. Additionally, nintedanib blocks non-receptor tyrosine kinases (eg, Src, Lck) directly, preventing the activation of fibroblasts. As a result, nintedanib ultimately inhibits fibroblast proliferation and migration, effectively attenuating angiogenesis within the lungs. This reduction in fibrosis and angiogenesis helps to impede the progression of ILD, IPF, and associated complications such as pulmonary hypertension.

Document [19] (Title: Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitors -- Contraindications) Data on TKI use during pregnancy is still lacking in most cases. As a result, TKIs are typically co-prescribed with an effective contraception method during therapy and several weeks after discontinuing the TKI. Other limitations to using TKIs include severe adverse reactions. Patients at particular risk for one of the known adverse effects of the TKIs about to be prescribed, such as hypertension, interstitial lung disease, and long-QT syndrome, should receive an alternative therapy if possible.

Document [24] (Title: Vemurafenib -- Indications) The kinase protein family, including MAP, RAS, RAF, MEK, and ERK, has a key role in regulating intracellular and extracellular signaling pathways that control cellular growth, differentiation, transformation, and apoptosis (programmed cell death). In normal cells, these kinases operate in a controlled cascade of phosphorylation and dephosphorylation in response to growth factors, eventually leading to the regulation of cellular growth and proliferation in an organized manner. However, mutations in the genes encoding these kinases can disrupt this regulation, resulting in uncontrolled cell growth, which can lead to the development of cancer.

Document [26] (Title: Lung Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Targeted Therapy for NSCLC) In the early 2000s, researchers discovered that specific mutations encode critical proteins for cell growth and replication. These mutations were named “driver mutations.” It was proposed that blocking these mutation pathways may improve survival in lung cancer patients. The current practice is to check for the following mutations in every advanced NSCLC. Each of these mutations has a specific inhibitor available: EGFR (epidermal growth factor receptor) is a mutation inhibited by tyrosine kinase inhibitors erlotinib, gefitinib, and afatinib. [36] ALK (Anaplastic lymphoma kinase) includes the specific inhibitors crizotinib, ceritinib, and alectinib. A structurally similar mutation is ROS-1. The FDA recently approved crizotinib for treating cancers expressing ROS-1 mutation.
Document [27] (Title: Pharmacogenomics Overview -- Clinical Significance -- Genes and Broad-Spectrum Drug Approvals) Tumor-agnostic approvals revolutionize cancer treatment by targeting specific genetic biomarkers regardless of tumor origin. These biomarkers include tumor mutational burden, mismatch repair deficiencies, microsatellite instability status, neurotrophic tyrosine receptor kinase gene fusions, rearranged during transfection fusions, and rapidly accelerated fibrosarcoma B-type mutations. This precision medicine approach enables therapies to address the genetic drivers of cancer, broadening treatment options and improving patient outcomes. FDA-approved agents are available for each of these actionable mutations, further advancing personalized oncology care ( Table 1. Genetic Biomarkers and FDA Broad-Spectrum Drug Approvals). Document [0] (Title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 70) BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties, include dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, comprise ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another protein kinase inhibitor subclass, consist of canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab. By blocking EGFR tyrosine‑kinase signaling, these agents disrupt tumor cell proliferation and survival and can modulate immune responses, providing antineoplastic and immunomodulating effects. Collectively, targeted inhibition of BCR‑ABL, BTK, and EGFR exemplifies precision oncology, linking specific molecular targets to distinct hematologic and solid malignancies and guiding patient‑centered cancer therapy.
Document [1] (Title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 81) Blood cancer (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم) encompasses leukemias, including lymphoid (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي) and myeloid (ابيضاض الدم النقوي) forms, with specific subtypes such as chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن) and acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). Targeted kinase inhibition is a central therapeutic strategy. BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—are employed, the first three for lymphoid leukemia and the latter three for myeloid leukemia. BTK inhibitors, namely ibrutinib and zanubrutinib, belong to the same protein‑kinase inhibitor class, with ibrutinib indicated for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Additional protein‑kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity include EGFR inhibitors canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, as well as cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin are specifically used in acute myeloid leukemia. Conventional cytotoxic and corticosteroid agents such as cytarabine, dexamethasone acetate, dexamethasone phosphate, dexamethasonisonikotinát, irinotecan hydrochloride, pipobroman, and prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, and prednisolone tebutate also have activity against various blood cancers. Dexamethasone acetate additionally serves in the management of septic shock. Conversely, viral agents such as human herpesvirus‑1 and the oncolytic therapy talimogene laherparepvec are contraindicated in patients with blood cancer. These interrelated disease classifications, drug classes, and contraindications illustrate the complexity of therapeutic decision‑making in hematologic malignancies.
Document [2] (Title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 75) BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subclass of protein‑kinase inhibitors classified as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, comprise dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي), whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي). Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein‑kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, include ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن). The “Other protein kinase inhibitors in ATC” group, encompassing cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin, belongs to the same protein‑kinase‑inhibitor class and is employed to inhibit kinase signaling in cancer and to modulate immune responses. Document [3] (Title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 214) The community describes blood cancer (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم) as a group of leukemic disorders comprising lymphoid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي) and myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي), which correspond to chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن) and acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), respectively. Targeted therapy relies heavily on protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, imatinib mesylát, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—block the BCR‑ABL tyrosine kinase; the former four are used for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase inhibitors ibrutinib and zanubrutinib also belong to this class, with ibrutinib approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Additional protein kinase inhibitors include EGFR inhibitors canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, as well as other ATC‑listed agents cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin are employed in acute myeloid leukemia. Corticosteroids such as dexamethasone acetate (used for blood cancer and septic shock), dexamethasone phosphate, prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, and prednisolone tebutate demonstrate efficacy in hematologic malignancies. Chemotherapeutic agents irinotecan hydrochloride and pipobroman are also utilized. Contraindicated interventions include infection with human herpesvirus 1 and oncolytic viral therapy talimogene laherparepvec, which may exacerbate the disease. Collectively, these agents illustrate the role of kinase‑targeted and immunomodulatory drugs in the comprehensive management of leukemic disorders.
Document [4] (Title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2916) Dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are both classified as BCR‑ABL inhibitors. As BCR‑ABL inhibitors, these agents specifically target the BCR‑ABL fusion protein, a constitutively active tyrosine kinase that drives the development of chronic myeloid leukemia and certain acute lymphoblastic leukemias. By inhibiting BCR‑ABL activity, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib disrupt downstream signaling pathways, reducing malignant cell proliferation and providing therapeutic benefit in BCR‑ABL–positive hematologic malignancies. Their shared classification highlights a common mechanism of action, while differences in potency, spectrum of activity, and side‑effect profiles inform clinicians’ choices of targeted therapy for patients with BCR‑ABL–driven disease.

Within this therapeutic class, zanubrutinib is a specific BTK inhibitor that exemplifies the targeted approach of these agents. By inhibiting BTK, these drugs are employed in the treatment of certain malignancies and immune‑mediated conditions, reflecting their role as both anticancer and immune‑modulating therapies.
Document [22] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1289) Ibrutinib is a targeted therapeutic agent that may be used to treat سرطـان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن (chronic lymphocytic leukemia), offering a pharmacologic option for managing this hematologic malignancy.
Document [23] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1290) Cabozantinib and gilteritinib are both classified as members of the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group designated “Other protein kinase inhibitors,” indicating that each drug belongs to this specific category of protein kinase–targeting agents. This classification reflects their shared mechanism of action as protein kinase inhibitors within the broader ATC system.
Document [24] (Title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 765) Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors are a class of targeted therapies that block the BTK enzyme, a key component of B‑cell receptor signaling. Ibrutinib and zanubrutinib are both members of this BTK inhibitor class, sharing the same mechanism of action. Ibrutinib may treat سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن (chronic lymphocytic leukemia) by inhibiting malignant B‑cell proliferation. Recognizing that both ibrutinib and zanubrutinib belong to the BTK inhibitor family informs clinicians when selecting appropriate targeted agents for patients with B‑cell malignancies such as chronic lymphocytic leukemia.

Document [26] (Title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 83) Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors constitute a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. Within this subclass, ibrutinib and zanubrutinib are recognized members, and ibrutinib has therapeutic activity against chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Another grouping, designated as other protein kinase inhibitors in ATC, also belongs to the broader class of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. Cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin are members of this ATC group, and both gilteritinib and midostaurin are indicated for the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia. Together, these kinase‑targeting drugs illustrate the role of protein kinase inhibition in the management of hematologic malignancies such as chronic lymphocytic leukemia and acute myeloid leukemia.
Document [27] (Title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 579) The BCR‑ABL inhibitor class comprises several agents, including dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib. Dasatinib, its anhydrous form, and imatinib are each used to treat lymphoid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي). Ponatinib, its hydrochloride salt, and asciminib are each used to treat myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي). Asciminib is also identified as a member of the BCR‑ABL inhibitor group. All of these drugs act by targeting the BCR‑ABL tyrosine‑kinase pathway, and their selection in clinical practice is guided by the specific type of leukemia being treated.
Document [28] (Title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2018) Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, and imatinib are classified as BCR‑ABL inhibitors. Document [29] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1286) Imatinib is a BCR‑ABL inhibitor, meaning it belongs to the class of drugs that specifically target BCR‑ABL fusion proteins. BCR‑ABL fusion proteins serve as the molecular target for BCR‑ABL inhibitors; they are oncogenic drivers created by the Philadelphia chromosome translocation and are central to the pathogenesis of chronic myeloid leukemia and certain acute lymphoblastic leukemias. By binding to and inhibiting these fusion proteins, imatinib and other BCR‑ABL inhibitors can suppress the abnormal signaling that promotes uncontrolled cell proliferation, thereby providing an effective targeted therapy for patients with these malignancies.
Document [30] (Title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 89) The compound 4‑aminopyrazolo(3,4‑d)pyrimidine is classified as an anticholesteremic agent, and within this anticholesteremic category a subcategory called hematologic agents (hematologické látky) is defined. Hematologic agents include drugs such as zanubrutinib, filgrastim, and protamines. Zanubrutinib, together with ibrutinib, belongs to the class of Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors; BTK inhibitors are themselves a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors that have antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity. Ibrutinib is indicated for the treatment of chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن). Filgrastim, also a hematologic agent, is used to stimulate white‑blood‑cell production, and protamines represent another type of hematologic agent. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors constitute another group of protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties. Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are examples of EGFR inhibitors. A broader grouping labeled “Other protein kinase inhibitors in ATC” also falls under the protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents class. This group includes cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin. Gilteritinib and midostaurin have therapeutic activity against acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). Overall, the taxonomy links several targeted therapies—BTK inhibitors, EGFR inhibitors, and other protein kinase inhibitors—to their respective clinical indications in hematologic malignancies such as chronic lymphocytic leukemia and acute myeloid leukemia, while also situating them within broader pharmacologic categories like anticholesteremic agents and protein kinase inhibitor classes.
Document [31] (Title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 240) BCR‑ABL Inhibitor is a class of targeted therapy that includes dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, imatinib mesylát, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib. Ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib are also members of the BCR‑ABL Inhibitor class and are indicated for the treatment of أبيضاض الدم النقوي (myeloid leukemia). Understanding these specific drug‑disease relationships helps clinicians select appropriate targeted therapy based on the leukemia subtype.
